data.title: South Korea's acting president vows to advance tariff negotiations with US, Yonhap reports
data.description: South Korea's acting President Han Duck-soo said on Thursday that the government would seek to advance negotiations with the U.S. over tariffs in a bid to shake off the burden, the Yonhap News Agency reported.

data.title: Malaysia Investor Sentiment Likely to Stay Fragile on Trade Policy UncertaintyMarket Talk
data.description: Unpredictable U.S. trade policy could weigh on global growth and sentiment, and investors in Malaysia should stay cautious, RHB analyst Alexander Chia says in a note. While Malaysia remains relatively resilient among emerging markets, investor sentiment is likely to stay fragile, he says. Chia advises investors to build a defensive portfolio with higher cash holdings in near term, while selectively accumulating quality domestic-focused stocks on weakness. He sees Trump's tariffs as transactional, allowing room for negotiation but keeping markets on edge. RHB lowers its end-2025 KLCI target to 1650 from 1750. The KLCI is 4.3% higher at 1460.20. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: South Korean Won Weakens
data.description:
The South Korean won depreciated past 1,460 per dollar as rising US-China trade tensions overshadowed President Trump’s 90-day tariff pause.

The pause included a reduced 10% tariff for countries that did not retaliate, including South Korea, replacing earlier rates of up to 25%.

However, Trump intensified pressure on China, raising tariffs on Chinese imports to 125% in response to Beijing’s retaliatory measures.

This weighed on the export-driven won, as South Korea is a major trading partner of China.

Meanwhile, the tariff reprieve has created an opportunity for negotiations, with South Korea's top trade envoy Cheong In-kyo stating that the country is actively seeking to reduce tariffs through negotiations.

Additionally, the Industry Ministry stated on Thursday that the government will swiftly devise support measures for the local chip industry to mitigate the impact of US tariffs on semiconductor imports, South Korea’s largest export sector.

data.title: China Producer Prices Fall the Most in 4 Months
data.description:
China's producer prices dropped by 2.5% yoy in March 2025, softer than market forecasts of a 2.3% decline, following a 2.2% fall in the previous month.

This marked the fastest decline since November 2024, amid further weak domestic demand on the mainland.

data.title: China Consumer Prices Drop for Second Straight Month
data.description:
China's consumer prices fell by 0.1% yoy in March 2025, missing market forecasts of a 0.1% increase and marking the second straight month of decline.

However, the latest result was much softer than a 0.7% decline in the previous month.

data.title: China's consumer prices extend declines in March
data.description:
China's consumer prices fell for the second straight month in March while producer deflation persisted, as caution grows over the economic outlook amid mounting tariff risks.

The consumer price index dropped 0.1% last month from a year earlier, a slower pace than February's 0.7% decline, National Bureau of Statistics data showed on Thursday. That was below a Reuters poll forecast for prices to remain flat.

CPI fell 0.4% month-on-month, against a 0.2% fall in February and missing an estimated 0.3% decline.

The producer price index declined 2.5% in March from a year earlier, deepening the 2.2% fall in February, and below the expected 2.3% drop.

data.title: Comforia Residential REIT Sets Interest Rate for Fixed-Rate Loan
data.description:
Comforia Residential REIT has finalized the interest rate for its fixed-rate borrowing totaling 1.55 billion yen, according to its Wednesday bourse filing.

The loan from the Development Bank of Japan will carry a 1.23875% rate, with a drawdown date of April 11, 2025, and repayment due April 11, 2030.

The unsecured, non-guaranteed loan will be repaid in a lump sum under the agreed terms.

data.title: Wildcat Resources Conducts Definitive Feasibility Study Testwork at Western Australia Lithium Project, Shares Jump 7%
data.description:
Wildcat Resources said it continued to advance its Tabba Tabba lithium project in Western Australia, and definitive feasibility study test work is underway, including the scaled-up whole of ore flotation test work, ore sorting, and vendor test work, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

Phase two preliminary feasibility study whole of ore flotation test work at the project delivered results consistent with phase one metallurgical testing, with spodumene concentrate at 5.60% grade of lithium oxide produced from 1.01% grade of lithium oxide composite ore feed, with an overall recovery of 84.5%.

It appointed engineering firm NewPro to complete a pre-feasibility study process plant engineering at the project, per the filing.

It is progressing with drill planning at its Bolt Cutter project in Western Australia to test a lithium anomaly. The firm also said that permitting was in progress for diamond drilling at the Mt. Adrah gold project in New South Wales.

Its shares jumped 7% in early trading on Thursday.

data.title: Australia's Expected Inflation Rate Accelerates to 4.2% in April
data.description:
Australia's expected inflation rate rose by 0.6 percentage points to 4.2% in April, according to a Thursday report from the Melbourne Institute.

The sharp changes in inflationary expectations in recent months reflect significant consumer uncertainty about the outlook, per the report.

Meanwhile, trimmed mean inflation expectations remain below their levels in the year-ago period, while expected wage growth continues to be relatively weak, the report said.

data.title: Chinese Shares Open Higher, Defying Tit-For-Tat Tariffs
data.description:
Chinese shares opened higher on Thursday despite the overnight tit-for-tat tariffs between the US and China.

The Shanghai Composite Index, the main gauge of Chinese stocks, rose 1.2% to 3226.06 at market open. The Shenzhen Component Index climbed 2.1% to 9,742.63. Both indices are on track to record their third straight day of gains.

Investors appeared unfazed despite a renewed exchange of tariff increases between the world's two largest economies.

Citing "the lack of respect that China has shown to the world's markets," Trump raised duties on Chinese imports late Wednesday in the US to 125% from 104%.

Notably, the US decided to pause its previously announced "retaliatory tariffs" on other countries.

Trump's latest action came after China responded to the 104% duties on Chinese goods with its own tariff increase on US imports to 84% from the earlier 34%.

Additionally, China has added six more US firms to its unreliable entity list and placed 12 US entities on its export control list.

China has also lodged a complaint against the US with the World Trade Organization, according to state-run Xinhua News Agency.

data.title: JB Hi-Fi's Retreat Lures Bull Ahead of Rate CutsMarket Talk
data.description: JB Hi-Fi shares' recent retreat from a record secures the Australian entertainment retailer a new bull at Bell Potter. Analyst Chami Ratnapala raises her recommendation to buy from hold, with the stock having retreated 16% between early February and the end of Monday's session. She makes no changes to her forecasts, but tells clients in a note that she expects JB Hi-Fi's 3Q trading update will show sales growth close to the level seen early in the period. She acknowledges headwinds from a weak Australian dollar, economic uncertainty and promotional activity, but sees further central-bank rate cuts supporting consumer demand from next month. Bell Potter keeps a A$99.00 target price on the stock, which is up 5.5% at A$95.32. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Posco Future M's Heavy Exposure to U.S. Offers Risks, OpportunitiesMarket Talk
data.description: Posco Future M's heavy exposure to the U.S. electric-vehicle market offers both risks and opportunities, Daiwa Capital analysts Henny Jung and Yoonki Bae write in a note. The South Korean EV battery-material supplier's cathode shipment volume could contract 40% in 2025, they say, given its amplified vulnerability to slower U.S. shipments of battery electric vehicles under potential policy changes by the new Trump administration. On the other hand, its anode-material business could expand faster than expected, thanks to clients' possible import-diversification efforts amid rising uncertainties over U.S. tariffs on China, which accounted for 40% of the 2024 U.S. anode-material imports, they add. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: Santana Minerals Says Drilling at New Zealand Gold Project Intersects New Gold Mineralization; Shares Jump 10%
data.description:
Santana Minerals said infill drilling at the Rise and Shine South orebody at its Bendigo-Ophir gold project in New Zealand intersected new gold mineralization, according to Thursday filings with the Australian and New Zealand bourses.

Four drill holes intersected new mineralization in the southwestern sector of the Rise and Shine deposit beneath the open pit shell defined in the November 2024 prefeasibility study for the project.

"Significant" intercepts included 33 meters at 2.7 grams per tonne (g/t) grade of gold from 98 meters, as well as 19 meters at 1.5 g/t grade of gold from 179 meters.

The southern extent of the deposit was sparsely drilled in the past due to terrain and access difficulties with the steep slope into the valley. Infill drilling is continuing, and it is expected to be completed in the coming weeks.

Santana Minerals' shares jumped 10% in recent trading on the Australian bourse and over 13% in recent trading on the New Zealand bourse on Thursday.

data.title: China Looks to Advance Talks With EU After Trade Call, Commerce Ministry Says
data.description:
China has pledged to accelerate talks with the European Union on various hot-button issues as Beijing looks to forge closer ties with trading partners in the face of escalating tensions with the U.S.

According to China's commerce ministry, Chinese Commerce Minister Wang Wentao held a video call with the European Commissioner for Trade and Economic Security, Maros Sefcovic, on Tuesday. During the call, the two sides discussed a range of bilateral trade issues--including electric vehicles--as well as President Trump's so-called reciprocal tariffs, the ministry said on Thursday.

Both officials have agreed to start consultations on market access, and to immediately conduct negotiations on EV price commitments, the Chinese ministry said. They also discussed cooperation on auto industry investment, pledging to support the restarting of a trade remedy dialogue mechanism, according to the ministry.

The call happened around the same time as Chinese Premier Li Qiang had a phone call with EU President Ursula von der Leyen, who during the talk mentioned the potential oversupply of cheap Chinese goods to the trading bloc as fresh U.S. tariffs disrupt global trade.

Minister Wang on Wednesday also held a talk with his Malaysian counterpart, Tengku Zafrul Aziz, on trade cooperation and tariff issues, the Chinese commerce ministry said.

Write to Singapore editors at singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: Indian financial markets closed on April 10 for holiday
data.description:
India's equity, currency and debt markets will be closed on Thursday, April 10, for a local holiday. Trading will resume on Friday, April 11.

The NSE Nifty 50 fell 0.61% while the BSE Sensex declined 0.51% on Wednesday, tracking a global selloff sparked by U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs. A widely-expected rate cut and dovish stance by the RBI failed to lift sentiment.

The Indian rupee dropped 0.5% versus the U.S. dollar to 86.6875, tracking weakness in the Chinese yuan as sweeping U.S. tariffs came into effect, including a 104% levy on China, and fuelled worries of a slowdown in global growth.

The benchmark 10-year bond (IN067934G=CC) was quoted at 102.4325 rupees, with the yield down 3 bps at 6.4432%, its lowest since December 2021, after the Reserve Bank of India's dovish monetary policy decision and guidance triggered fresh investor purchases.

data.title: China lowers yuan midpoint fixing for sixth straight day to fresh 19-month low
data.description:
China lowered its official yuan midpoint for the six straight day to a fresh 19-month low against the dollar on Thursday.

Prior to market opening, the People's Bank of China (PBOC) set the midpoint rate , around which the yuan is allowed to trade in a 2% band, at 7.2092 per dollar, the weakest since September 11, 2023 but 1,392 pips firmer than a Reuters' estimate of 7.3484.

The PBOC has slightly loosened its grip on the currency this week by allowing official guidance to weaken past the key threshold of 7.2 per dollar level, although it came in much stronger than market projections, in what traders and analysts interpreted as an official attempt to keep the yuan steady.

The PBOC's focus on steady yuan moves comes even as the worsening U.S. trade war severely challenges the competitiveness of China's massive export sector, suggesting financial market stability remains the priority.
